title: EU agrees to slim down AI Act and ban nudification apps
subtitle: The Council and Parliament closed the AI Omnibus deal on May 7 — pushing back high-risk and watermarking deadlines while adding a hard ban on AI systems that generate non-consensual intimate imagery.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On May 7, the EU Council and Parliament reached political agreement on the AI Omnibus — the package amending the 2024 AI Act. The deal pushes the application date for Annex 3 high-risk systems (employment, education, health insurance, and similar) from this summer to December 2 2027, and extends the watermarking transparency obligation to December 2 2026. It also adds an outright ban, applicable from December this year, on AI systems that generate non-consensual sexually explicit or intimate content, including child sexual abuse material — covering both providers who place such systems on the market without safety controls and deployers who use them.

The deadline shifts come from industry and Member State pressure that the original timelines were unworkable; the nudification ban comes from a wave of Grok-generated sexual deepfakes last winter that the Parliament treated as proof that downstream content moderation cannot, on its own, address harms baked into the model layer. Article 5 of the AI Act already prohibited certain practices; the omnibus extends that prohibition to a category of generative output explicitly, and pushes the obligation upstream to the model provider.

This is the first substantive amendment to the AI Act since it entered into force, and the negotiating dynamic is as informative as the text. Digital-strategy commissioners pitched the omnibus as 'simplification.' Rapporteurs and several Member States insisted it could not pass without new prohibitions attached. The result — looser timelines for industry, harder bans on a narrow class of generative misuse — is roughly the trade the Parliament has been telegraphing since the GPAI code of practice debate in late 2025.

For learners: regulation is rarely a single decision; it is a sequence of compromises that move the bar in opposite directions at once. The EU's AI Act is now easier to comply with for general high-risk uses and harder to ignore for a specific harm. Watching which deadlines slip and which prohibitions tighten tells you which arguments — economic burden vs. concrete harm — are landing with legislators in any given quarter.
